coredata.dc:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of Na-doped carbon dots for highly selective detection of Hg2+ in aqueous medium
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Yanxi
Zhao, Yafeng
Xia, Xiaofeng
Cao, Mingjun
Yan, Mengjie
Jiang, Lin
Su, Yaxin
Li, Hongqi
coredata.dc:description: Na-doped carbon dots (Na-CDs) was synthesized by hydrothermal method from β-cyclodextrin and sodium acetate. The Na-CDs was stable at pH 3–12 and after storage for a long time. The Na-CDs served fluorescence sensor for highly selective detection of Hg2+ in aqueous medium with a detection limit of 34.22 nM. The sensing mechanism was based on the aggregation caused fluorescence quenching. The Na-CDs displayed high selectivity and sensitivity towards Hg2+, which supplied a new idea for constructing CDs-based stable fluorescent probes and a novel platform for selective Hg2+ detection.

Luminescence
Optical materials and properties
Sensors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of pH (a) and storage temperature (b) on the stability of the Na-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of Na-CDs before and after addition of different metal cations. (b) Fluorescence intensity of Na-CDs (black bar), Na-CDs/different metal ions (grey bars), and Na-CDs/Hg2+/other metal cations (purple bars). (c) Fluorescence spectra of Na-CDs with different concentrations of Hg2+. (d) Relationship between fluorescence intensity of Na-CDs/Hg2+ and Hg2+ concentration. (e) Fluorescence intensity changes of Na-CDs/Hg2+ with time.

head.ce:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of Na-doped carbon dots for highly selective detection of Hg2+ in aqueous medium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Donghua University, Shanghai, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Donghua University, Shanghai, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Donghua University, Shanghai, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Donghua University, Shanghai, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Na-doped carbon dots (Na-CDs) was synthesized by hydrothermal method from β-cyclodextrin and sodium acetate. The Na-CDs was stable at pH 3–12 and after storage for a long time. The Na-CDs served fluorescence sensor for highly selective detection of Hg2+ in aqueous medium with a detection limit of 34.22 nM. The sensing mechanism was based on the aggregation caused fluorescence quenching. The Na-CDs displayed high selectivity and sensitivity towards Hg2+, which supplied a new idea for constructing CDs-based stable fluorescent probes and a novel platform for selective Hg2+ detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Accumulation of Hg2+ in bodies could lead to cellular abnormalities and diseases. So efficient determination of Hg2+ is significant. Recently fluorescent carbon dots attracted wide attention due to their multiple advantages [1] and a great number of carbon dots-based fluorometric sensors for Hg2+ have been reported [2–5]. Doping of carbon dots is regarded as an effective way to adjust the surface defects and to improve the qunatum yields, and the doping atoms are non-metal atoms such as N and S and/or metal ions [6–8]. The CDs-based fluorescent probes for Hg2+ sensing display shortcomings such as relatively poor stability, narrow applicable pH range, and complex synthesis at 400 ℃ or the utilization of toxic and/or expensive reagents [4,5,9]. Considering probes synthesis and their sensing applications, it is challenging yet highly desired to achieve convenient and green synthesis of CDs with high stability for selective detection of Hg2+ over a broad pH range from non-toxic, inexpensive raw materials.

We design to synthesize Na-CDs by hydrothermal reaction of β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) and sodium acetate. It is envisaged that β-CD may provide rich oxygenated groups, remarkable recognition capability, good biocompatibility, and high stability to the carbon dots after conversion [10]. Sodium acetate can provide plenty of oxygen-based functional groups and sufficient Na-doping, which may passivate the electronegative oxygen related defects and create new Na–O related defect centers in the band gap, resulting in deep blue photoluminescence with hugely reduced band width and remarkably enhanced quantum yield [11]. Considering that Hg2+ was prone to strong interaction with oxygen-containing groups on the surface of carbon dots [12,13], the Na-CDs prepared from β-CD and sodium acetate may exhibit high selectivity and good immunity to interference for fluorescent detection of Hg2+.

All reagents were of high purity of 99.9 %. The morphology was observed on FEI Talos F200S transmission electron microscopy (TEM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by Thermofisher escalab 250xi. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was obtained using D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer. Particle size and zeta potential were acquired on 90Plus PALS. UV–vis spectra were measured using U-2910 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were measured on F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Na-CDs was synthesized as showed in Fig. S1.

TEM images of Na-CDs (Fig. S2(a) and (b)) revealed that Na-CDs was spherical, uniformly dispersed and had no obvious aggregation. Particle size of Na-CDs was distributed between 1 and 10 nm (Fig. S2(c)). XRD of Na-CDs (Fig. S2(d)) and the (002) peak at 21.7° indicated high degree of disorder of carbon atoms. Zeta potential of Na-CDs was –31.17 mV, indicting that the surface of Na-CDs was negatively charged and there were carboxyl or hydroxyl groups on the surface [14]. XPS tests (Fig. S3) revealed the existence of C–C, C–O, C = O, and C–O–C/O–H bonds in Na-CDs. The faint peak at 1071.52 eV in Na1s spectrum (Fig. S3(d)) demonstrated the presence of sodium in Na-CDs [15]. UV–vis spectra of Na-CDs showed a spike at 290 nm (Fig. S4(a)). This peak was attributed to π-π* transition of C = C bond, consistent with previous report [16]. The raw materials were non-fluorescent while Na-CDs displayed a strong fluorescence peak centered 432 nm (Fig. S4(b)). The optimal excitation and emission wavelength for Na-CDs was 340 and 432 nm, respectively (Fig. S4(c) and S4(d)).

Effect of different pH (3–12) on the fluorescence intensity of Na-CDs (Fig. 1 (a)) revealed that Na-CDs exhibited the highest fluorescence intensity at pH 6–7 and was stable at pH 3–12. Compared with other reported carbon dots [17–19], Na-CDs showed excellent acid and alkali resistance. Considering the actual application, pH 7 was selected as the test condition for subsequent experiments. Fluorescence intensity of Na-CDs stored at room temperature for 3 h decreased by just 5.69 % compared to that of Na-CDs stored at a low temperature of 4 ℃ (Fig. 1(b)), indicating that Na-CDs was stable at room temperature for a long time, which facilitates the practical utility of Na-CDs in real environments.

The quantum yield of the developed Na-CDs was found to be approximately 15.8 % (quinine sulfate was used as the reference). Fluorescence spectra of Na-CDs before and after addition of different metal ions (Li+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Mg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+ and Al3+) as showed in Fig. 2 (a) indicated that only Hg2+ could cause significant decrease in fluorescence while other metal ions had no substantial effect on fluorescence of Na-CDs. Addition of different common anions (NO3 –, Cl–, Br–, I–, SO4 2– and SO3 2–) did not bring about marked changes in the fluorescence emission of Na-CDs (Fig. S5 and Fig. S6). So Na-CDs could be used for selective sensing of Hg2+ in aqueous medium. Anti-interference investigation based on fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2(b)) showed that competitive metal ions did not affect fluorescence intensity of Na-CDs distinctly, namely coexisting metal ions did not interfere with the sensing even though a variety of metal ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+ and Ca2+) are simultaneously present (Fig. S7 and Fig. S8) and Na-CDs could be used for fluorescence detection of Hg2+ in the presence of competitive metal ions. Fluorescence spectra of Na-CDs after adding different concentrations of Hg2+ (Fig. 2(c)) indicated that the fluorescence intensity of Na-CDs decreased gradually as Hg2+ concentration increased from 4 to 20 μM. The fluorescence intensity of Na-CDs/Hg2+ had a linear relationship with the concentration of Hg2+ (Fig. 2(d)) and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 34.22 nM.

Experimental results on response time of Na-CDs toward Hg2+ (Fig. 2(e)) showed that the fluorescence intensity of Na-CDs decreased sharply within 10 min upon addition of Hg2+, indicating the rapid response of Na-CDs to Hg2+. Then the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased, approaching equilibrium in around 90 min and remained constant in the subsequent time. This indicated that Na-CDs could be used for detecting Hg2+ rapidly and for continuous detection over a long period of time.

Compared with the particle size of Na-CDs (4.5 nm), the value of Na-CDs after addition of Hg2+ (Fig. S9(a)) increased remarkably and was concentrated at 20 nm, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching of Na-CDs by Hg2+ was ascribed to the aggregate caused quenching (ACQ) principle, as observed in other carbon dots for fluorescence turn-off sensing of Hg2+ [12,13,16]. Zeta potential of Na-CDs before and after addition of Hg2+ (Fig. S9(b)) showed that addition of Hg2+ reduced the zeta potential of Na-CDs solution from 31.17 mV to 26.10 mV, suggesting aggregate formation of Na-CDs induced by Hg2+. Possible sensing mechanism of Na-CDs for detection of Hg2+ was proposed (Fig. S10). Hg2+ chelated with functional groups on the surface of Na-CDs, caused Na-CDs to aggregate, and led to increase in the particle size and fluorescence quenching based on the ACQ mechanism. The chelation of Hg2+ with Na-CDs was further supported by the absorption spectra of Na-CDs before and after addition of Hg2+ ions (Fig. S11), which revealed remarkable changes in the absorption due to the interaction between Hg2+ and Na-CDs. Comparison of Na-CDs with other CDs-based Hg2+ fluorescent probes (Table S1) revealed high stability at pH 3–12, a relatively low detection limit of 34.22 nM, and facile synthesis by hydrothermal method from cheap raw materials β-CD and sodium acetate of Na-CDs. The developed Na-CDs may have great potential for practical Hg2+ detection in aqueous medium.

Na-CDs was prepared from β-CD and sodium acatate and was stable at pH 3–12 and after storage at room temperature. Fluorescence of Na-CDs could be dramatically reduced by Hg2+ and the selective sensing of Hg2+ was uninterrupted by competitive metal ions and anions. The detection limit was calculated to be 34.22 nM. Studies suggested that Hg2+ could chelate with oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of Na-CDs, caused formation of aggregate and fluorescence quenching of Na-CDs. It was demonstrated that Na-doping was an effective pathway for constructing stable fluorescent CDs for selective detection of heavy metal ions in real environments.

Yanxi Song: Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Yafeng Zhao: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xiaofeng Xia: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Mingjun Cao: Investigation, Data curation. Mengjie Yan: Methodology, Data curation. Lin Jiang: Investigation, Methodology. Yaxin Su: Resources, Conceptualization. Hongqi Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 52276103 to Yaxin Su) is acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2023.135412.
